summary: Adversarial /er evidence review + code review of ez-gh-actions v1

er.reasoning: The verdict is forced by a confirmed critical evidence-integrity failure, verified firsthand by the judge: `sha256sum -c checksums.sha256` in /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/evidence/e2e-20260703 exits 1 because git_sha.txt is listed in the manifest (hash be8f6a12...) but absent from the bundle — 10 of 11 manifest entries verify, the 11th is missing. The review rules state that a checksum failure and a confirmed critical evidence-integrity finding each independently mandate FAIL. The missing file is specifically the artifact that pins the bundle to the commit under test, compounding the severity. This FAIL is an integrity verdict, not a fabrication verdict: every substantive claim was independently re-verified live against GitHub with zero mismatches (run 28685531107 completed/success on head_sha 5ef5406..., server-side job log contains the container hostname 1143cf62e74f matching the local container ID, memory.max=6267338752=5977 MiB exactly, pids.max=512, runners endpoint shows total_count=0, and the last 3 CI runs are all green including commits 5ef5406 and c27d389). Fixing the manifest (restore git_sha.txt or regenerate checksums.sha256) would make this a PASS-quality bundle. Per the rules, the confirmed critical code bugs do not fail the evidence claims but must be recorded: (1) free_disk_gb() runs host-side df against the daemon's DockerRootDir path, so the disk-floor guard silently fails open on Colima/Docker Desktop — the exact disk-fill incident class the tool exists to prevent; (2) ezgha stop's stop_all() deregisters ALL idle ezgha-* runners on the target, including other hosts' runners in org scope — a fleet-wide outage button. Confirmed majors additionally include: `docker ps --format json` breaking on Docker CLI <23.0, unbounded JIT-registration leak from crash-looping --rm containers, stop racing the serve service (runners resurrect within 30s), non-target-scoped managed label miscounting multi-config capacity, missing --cap-drop ALL / egress restriction / read-only rootfs, dash-prefixed image value parsed as a docker flag defeating hardening, and JIT bearer credential exposure via argv/docker inspect. Minor evidence caveats: the 'failure-path cleanup verified' narrative in EVIDENCE.md has no backing artifact, and the --cpus limit is not provable in-container via nproc (memory and pids limits ARE proven inside the job).

claim_table.claim: Bundle integrity: checksums.sha256 verifies cleanly (implicit precondition for all evidence)
claim_table.evidence: Judge-verified firsthand: `sha256sum -c checksums.sha256` exits 1 — `git_sha.txt: FAILED open or read`. Manifest lists 11 files, only 10 exist. The missing file is the commit-pinning artifact (hash be8f6a12... present in manifest). Per review rules, this alone forces FAIL.

claim_table.claim: (1) ezgha starts a real ephemeral JIT runner with hard cgroup limits
claim_table.evidence: Non-circular provenance: container ID 1143cf62e74f appears in local 04_status_before_job.txt AND as hostname in GitHub's server-side job log (07_job_log.txt), re-confirmed via live API (runner_name ezgha-Jeff-Ubuntu-6a4833a11c652b, labels [self-hosted, ezgha]). Caveat: memory/pids cgroup limits proven in-container; --cpus limit only asserted via source flags (nproc cannot prove it).

claim_table.claim: (2) A real GitHub Actions job executed on it
claim_table.evidence: Judge live-verified: gh api run 28685531107 → status=completed, conclusion=success, event=workflow_dispatch, head_sha=5ef5406779925598565c8d277d13b865db78947a, matching EVIDENCE.md's commit under test. GitHub's own job log timestamps (22:12:26-27Z) order correctly with run start (22:12:21Z) and bundle write (~22:14 UTC).

claim_table.claim: (3) The runner deregistered and container cleaned up after the job
claim_table.evidence: Judge live-verified: gh api .../actions/runners → total_count=0, no lingering runners. 08_status_after_job.txt shows 0 containers / 0 registered runners. Caveat: the separate 'failure-path cleanup verified' narrative line in EVIDENCE.md has no backing artifact (minor, does not affect this claim).

claim_table.claim: (4) Limits were enforced (memory.max/pids.max inside the job)
claim_table.evidence: GitHub's server-side job log shows cgroup memory.max=6267338752 (= 5977 MiB * 1048576 exactly, matching daemon-derived 11955/2 design) and pids.max=512 (matching config.toml and 01_init.txt), read from inside the running job container.
Judge live-verified: last 3 ci.yml runs on jleechanorg/ez-gh-actions all conclusion=success, covering headShas 537e1e5, c27d389 (mid-run daemon-capacity fix), and 5ef5406 (commit under test).

claim_table.claim: Code quality of the system under test (context, not an evidence claim)
claim_table.evidence: Confirmed criticals: disk-floor guard fails open on Colima/Docker Desktop (df on host vs daemon's DockerRootDir, docker_backend.rs:159-181); stop_all() deregisters other hosts' ezgha runners in org scope (docker_backend.rs:148-154). Confirmed majors: docker ps --format json breaks on CLI <23.0; unbounded JIT-registration leak on crash-looping containers; stop/serve race resurrects runners in ≤30s; non-target-scoped ezgha=managed label; missing --cap-drop ALL/egress/read-only rootfs; dash-prefixed image value defeats hardening flags; JIT credential in argv. Per rules these do NOT fail the evidence claims.

confirmed.title: sha256sum -c fails: git_sha.txt listed in checksums.sha256 but missing from bundle
confirmed.detail: Running `cd /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/evidence/e2e-20260703 && sha256sum -c checksums.sha256` exits 1 with `git_sha.txt: FAILED open or read`. The checksum manifest lists 11 files but only 10 exist. git_sha.txt (hash be8f6a12...) was presumably the artifact pinning the bundle to commit 5ef5406779925598565b78947a and was never committed or was deleted after the manifest was written. Per /er rules, any checksum failure caps the bundle at INVALID/incomplete regardless of other quality. Mitigation found during audit: the live GitHub API (repos/jleechanorg/ez-gh-actions/actions/runs/28685531107) independently reports head_sha=5ef5406779925598565b78947a... matching EVIDENCE.md's claimed commit, so the underlying claim is TRUE — but the bundle itself does not carry that proof and fails its own integrity check.
confirmed.recommendation: Restore git_sha.txt (content: the commit SHA, e.g. output of `git rev-parse HEAD` at capture time) so its hash matches be8f6a12f958fa19c8e55b30ceddbda44f5374d14236c1fad6bd297ff7b87622, or regenerate checksums.sha256 to match the actual file set and re-verify. Do not ship a bundle whose manifest references absent files.

verdict.reasoning: Attempted refutation failed on every axis. (1) The failure is real and reproducible: sha256sum -c in /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/evidence/e2e-20260703 exits 1 with 'git_sha.txt: FAILED open or read'; the manifest lists 11 files, only 10 exist. (2) Git history confirms the mechanism: evidence commit 537e1e5 added 11 files including checksums.sha256 but never git_sha.txt, and no commit in any branch ever contained git_sha.txt — it was hashed into the manifest from the working tree and never committed, exactly as the finding hypothesized. (3) The finding's own mitigation is accurate: live gh api for run 28685531107 returns head_sha=5ef5406779925598565c8d277d13b865db78947a matching EVIDENCE.md's claimed commit, so the underlying provenance claim is true but the bundle does not carry that proof and fails its own integrity check. (4) Severity is consistent with the operative /er rule that any sha256sum -c failure caps the bundle at INVALID regardless of other quality; only cosmetic issue is a truncated SHA transcription in the finding summary, which does not affect correctness. Finding stands.

confirmed.title: free_disk_gb() checks the HOST filesystem at the DAEMON's path — disk-floor guard silently fails open in exactly the VM setups the tool targets
confirmed.detail: docker_backend.rs:159-181. `free_disk_gb()` asks the daemon for `{{.DockerRootDir}}` (a path INSIDE the daemon's mount namespace, e.g. `/var/lib/docker` inside the Colima/Lima/Docker Desktop VM) and then runs host-side `df -Pk` against that string. Three concrete failures: (1) macOS Docker Desktop / Colima: `/var/lib/docker` does not exist on the host, `df` exits non-zero, function returns `None`, and `ensure_count` (docker_backend.rs:192) SKIPS the disk check entirely — the guard the code comments call 'the dominant self-hosted runner failure mode' is a silent no-op. (2) Linux host that also has a native `/var/lib/docker` directory but uses a Colima context: `df` measures the host root fs (plenty free) while the VM disk is 100% full — guard passes, spawns more runners onto a full VM disk, jobs fail with ENOSPC. This is precisely the 2026-06-14 colima disk-fill incident class. (3) daemon_capacity() at docker_backend.rs:36 already documents that the daemon may be a VM, so the codebase knows about this and still gets it wrong for disk.
confirmed.recommendation: Query disk through the daemon, not the host: `docker system df --format` or run `df` inside a throwaway container (`docker run --rm alpine df -Pk /`), or read `docker info` disk fields where available. At minimum, treat `None` from free_disk_gb() as a hard error (fail closed) instead of skipping the check, since the guard is the product's core promise.

verdict.reasoning:
NOT REFUTED — confirmed with code reading plus a live reproduction on the exact host class the tool's own evidence bundle used.

Factual basis verified line-by-line:
1. /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/docker_backend.rs:159-181 — `free_disk_gb()` gets `{{.DockerRootDir}}` from `docker info` (a daemon-namespace path) and runs host-side `df -Pk` against that string; on df failure it returns `None`.
2. docker_backend.rs:192-200 — `ensure_count` uses `if let Some(free) = free_disk_gb()`, so `None` silently skips the disk floor entirely (fail-open), while the function's own comment (lines 184-186) calls disk exhaustion "the dominant self-hosted runner failure mode."
3. docker_backend.rs:33-35 — `daemon_capacity()`'s doc comment explicitly acknowledges the daemon may live in a Colima/Lima/Docker Desktop VM; CPU/memory are daemon-derived but disk is not, exactly as the finding says.

Live reproduction of failure mode (2), on this machine (same host class as the evidence bundle's "Jeff-Ubuntu ... docker daemon = 4-cpu/12GB Lima VM"): active docker context is `lima-colima`; `docker info` reports DockerRootDir=/var/lib/docker (Ubuntu 24.04 VM daemon); the host ALSO has a native root-owned /var/lib/docker; host `df -Pk /var/lib/docker` succeeds and reports the HOST root fs: ~497 GB free (41% used). Inside the VM, `limactl shell colima -- df -Pk /var/lib/docker` shows /dev/vda1 with only ~28 GB free (76% used). So free_disk_gb() currently overreports free space by ~470 GB on the very machine the e2e evidence (run 28685531107) validated — if the VM disk filled, the guard would still pass and spawn runners. This matches the 2026-06-14 colima disk-fill incident class.

Failure mode (1) (macOS Docker Desktop/Colima) also holds by inspection: /var/lib/docker does not exist on a macOS host, `df` exits non-zero, `free_disk_gb()` returns None, and ensure_count skips the check silently. macOS is an explicitly supported platform (src/platform.rs detects target_os = "macos"; README/DESIGN list Tart/macOS on the roadmap), and Docker on macOS always runs in a VM, so the guard is a guaranteed no-op there.

Severity is not inflated: the guard's sole purpose (per the code's own comment) is the dominant failure mode, and it is either a silent no-op (macOS) or measures the wrong filesystem (Linux+Colima — the tool's own validated deployment environment). Critical/high is justified.

confirmed.title: stop_all() deregisters OTHER hosts' ezgha runners — org/multi-host `ezgha stop` is a fleet-wide outage button
confirmed.detail: docker_backend.rs:148-154. After removing local containers, `stop_all` lists ALL runners on the target and removes every one whose name starts with `ezgha-` and `!r.busy`. Runner names are `ezgha-{hostname}-{suffix}` (docker_backend.rs:67), so with org scope (or a shared repo) host A's `ezgha stop` deregisters host B's and host C's idle runners too. Concrete scenario: 3 hosts each serving `count=2` against org `jleechanorg`; operator runs `ezgha stop` on host A to take it down for maintenance → all 6 idle registrations are deleted; hosts B/C's waiting listeners are severed server-side, their containers exit, and queued jobs sit with zero available runners until B/C's 30s serve loops re-register. Repeated stops cause continuous org-wide runner churn. Additionally the busy check is a stale snapshot: a runner can pick up a job between list_runners() and remove_runner() (GitHub returns 422 for busy runners, which mitigates but produces spurious errors that are silently swallowed by `let _ =`).
confirmed.recommendation: Filter by this host's prefix: `r.name.starts_with(&format!("ezgha-{}-", hostname()))`. Better: record the runner_id label (`ezgha.runner_id` is already set on containers at docker_backend.rs:76) and deregister exactly the ids belonging to containers this host just removed.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against actual code, evidence bundle, and live gh API. (1) docker_backend.rs:141-156 stop_all removes every runner named ezgha-* with !busy from the configured target with no hostname scoping, despite names embedding hostname (line 67) — cited lines exact. (2) github.rs api_base maps org scope to orgs/{target}, so list/remove operate org-wide. (3) Live query of orgs/jleechanorg/actions/runners returned 21 runners spanning multiple physical hosts in one flat list, confirming host A sees and can delete host B/C registrations at org scope. (4) Evidence file 04_status_before_job.txt shows a real idle JIT runner listed as busy=false, confirming idle JIT registrations match the deletion predicate. (5) main.rs:210 confirms the 30s serve loop; hosts running one-shot `ezgha start` never self-heal, making the impact potentially worse than stated. (6) The TOCTOU busy-snapshot claim is accurate and the 422 is swallowed by `let _ =` at line 151. Minor severity nuance: in serve mode the outage is transient (~30s, jobs queue rather than fail), so "outage button" is worst-case framing — but severity inflation alone does not refute, and the unsupervised-start case is persistent capacity loss. Factual basis fully confirmed; not refuted.

confirmed.title: `docker ps --format json` is a literal Go template on Docker CLI < 23.0 — every container operation permanently fails
confirmed.detail: docker_backend.rs:116-128. On Docker CLI 20.10 (Ubuntu 22.04 / Debian 11 `docker.io` package, still widely deployed on the exact commodity hosts this tool targets) `--format json` is interpreted as a literal template and prints the string `json` once per container. `serde_json::from_str("json")` then fails with 'unexpected docker ps json', so `managed_containers()` errors → `ensure_count`, `stop_all`, and `Status` are ALL broken. In `serve` this manifests as an infinite 'ensure_count failed (will retry)' loop every 30s that never starts a single runner; `ezgha stop` errors out and removes nothing. The shorthand `--format json` was only added in CLI v23.0 (before that you must write `--format '{{json .}}'`). Verified NDJSON output on local 29.4.3; the pre-23 literal behavior is the documented CLI change. Related fragility in github.rs:41: `-f "labels[]=x"` array syntax requires gh >= 2.27; on older gh it sends a scalar field literally named `labels[]` and generate-jitconfig 422s on every spawn attempt.
confirmed.recommendation: Use `--format '{{json .}}'` which works on all CLI versions that support formatting, or fail fast with a version check in `doctor`/`platform::detect()` (minimum docker CLI 23.0, minimum gh 2.27) so the operator gets one clear error instead of a silent retry loop.

verdict.reasoning:
NOT REFUTED. The core factual basis is confirmed at every level I could check.

CONFIRMED:
1. Code citation exact: /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/docker_backend.rs:116-128 runs `docker ps --filter label=ezgha=managed --format json` and parses each output line with `serde_json::from_str(...).context("unexpected docker ps json")`. `ensure_count` (line 188), `stop_all` (line 142), and `Status` (main.rs:220) all call `managed_containers()`, and serve's loop (main.rs:201-211) prints "ensure_count failed (will retry)" every 30s on error — exactly as claimed.
2. Docker version fact verified against docker/cli source via live gh queries: `cli/command/formatter/formatter.go` at tag v20.10.24 contains NO `json` alias handling at all, while v23.0.0 has `JSONFormatKey = "json"` → `finalFormat = "{{json .}}"`. On 20.10 the argument `json` is a literal Go template, which prints the string "json" once per matching container — this exact symptom is documented in docker/cli issue #4070 ("docker ps --format json does not work, it prints 'json' for every line"). `serde_json::from_str("json")` fails, so `managed_containers()` errors whenever >=1 managed container is running.
3. README documents no minimum Docker version ("prerequisites: docker daemon, gh CLI authenticated"), so the pre-23 scenario is not excluded by the tool's stated requirements, and the tool explicitly targets commodity self-hosted boxes.
4. If anything the window is WIDER than claimed: issue #4070 was filed against 23.0 itself, so early 23.0.x was also affected.

INACCURACIES FOUND (detail-level, not refutation):
1. Severity is somewhat inflated in the failure narrative: "never starts a single runner" / "every container operation permanently fails" is wrong. With ZERO managed containers, `docker ps --format json` on 20.10 prints nothing, `managed_containers()` returns Ok(empty), and `ensure_count` successfully starts the full runner complement (serve's first tick works; `ezgha start` works from a clean state). The parse failure only manifests once >=1 managed container is alive — then top-up, `status`, and `stop` all break. Because JIT runners self-remove after one job (`--rm`), the pool can drain to zero and serve recovers on that tick, so serve degrades (no partial top-up, spurious errors, broken stop/status while runners are live) rather than being totally inert. Still a real major defect: `ezgha stop` fails precisely when there is something to stop, and steady-state supervision is broken.
2. The secondary gh claim is factually wrong on the version: `-f "labels[]=x"` array-field syntax was added by cli/cli PR #6614 (merged 2022-12-22) and first shipped in gh v2.21.0 — verified via `gh api repos/cli/cli/compare/`: the merge commit is absent from v2.20.0 and present in v2.21.0. The finding's "gh >= 2.27" likely confuses this with GET query-param array support (#7513, ~v2.30). The fragility itself is real for gh < 2.21 (e.g. Ubuntu 22.04's apt-packaged gh 2.4.0), just with a different cutoff.

VERDICT: The primary finding (docker <23 breaks managed_containers and everything downstream) is factually correct, occurs on undocumented-but-targeted host configurations, and is fairly rated major. The "never starts a single runner" claim and the gh 2.27 cutoff are inaccurate details; per the instructions, severity/detail inflation alone does not refute.

confirmed.title: Crash-looping runner container leaks unbounded JIT registrations — no backoff, no visibility, no cleanup
confirmed.detail: docker_backend.rs:66-102, 187-207. `start_one` only cleans up the server-side JIT registration when `docker run` itself exits non-zero (line 91-98). If `docker run -d` succeeds but the container dies immediately afterwards — wrong-arch image on Apple Silicon, image missing `./run.sh`, expired/garbled jitconfig, OOM during runner bootstrap — the `--rm` flag erases the container, `managed_containers()` (docker ps, no `-a`) can never observe that it existed, and the offline JIT registration is orphaned on GitHub. The serve loop then sees `alive=0` and spawns a fresh registration+container every 30 seconds with zero backoff: ~2,880 orphaned offline `ezgha-*` registrations per day per configured runner, plus continuous generate-jitconfig API calls burning rate limit. `Status` shows the zombie list growing but nothing reaps them; only a manual `ezgha stop` (which also nukes other hosts, see separate finding) cleans up. The failure is silent from serve's perspective — it prints 'respawned ephemeral runner X' every cycle as if things were healthy.
confirmed.recommendation: (1) Drop `--rm` and use `docker ps -a` + explicit reaping so exited containers are observable and their `ezgha.runner_id` labels can drive targeted deregistration. (2) Add exponential backoff / a circuit breaker when a runner dies within N seconds of spawn. (3) Periodically reconcile: list `ezgha-{hostname}-*` runners that are offline with no corresponding container and remove them.

verdict.reasoning: Code inspection confirms the finding. src/docker_backend.rs:90-99 cleans up the JIT registration only when `docker run` exits non-zero; `docker run -d --rm` (line 73) means a container that starts then dies is auto-removed and invisible to managed_containers() (docker ps without -a, lines 116-126). The only remove_runner callers are the synchronous start failure path and stop_all — no reaper in the serve loop. src/main.rs:201-211 confirms a fixed 30s loop with no backoff that calls ensure_count → start_one → generate_jitconfig each cycle, printing 'respawned ephemeral runner X' as if healthy; 2,880 registrations/day arithmetic is correct. Two non-refuting caveats: (1) the 'missing ./run.sh' trigger example would actually fail synchronously and be cleaned up, but garbled/expired jitconfig, bootstrap failure, and OOM-kill are valid success-then-death triggers; (2) 'unbounded' is mildly overstated since GitHub auto-removes never-connected JIT registrations after ~1 day, giving a rolling steady state of ~2,880 zombies per runner rather than infinite growth — and list_runners' unpaginated per_page=100 means Status shows at most 100 of them. Severity nuance only; the core defect stands.

confirmed.title: `ezgha stop` races the serve loop and does not stop the installed service — runners resurrect within 30 seconds
confirmed.detail: main.rs:192-217, service.rs. `InstallService` installs a systemd --user unit with `Restart=on-failure` / launchd `KeepAlive=true` running `ezgha serve`, whose loop calls `ensure_count` every 30s forever (main.rs:201-211). `Commands::Stop` only calls `stop_all()` — it never touches the service or signals the serve process. Concrete scenario: operator installed the service, later runs `ezgha stop` during an incident (e.g. the disk is full and they want runners GONE); the command prints 'removed N managed container(s)' and exits 0, and ≤30s later the serve daemon re-registers and re-spawns everything. During an actual disk-exhaustion incident the disk-floor bail in ensure_count may prevent respawn, but for every other stop reason (security incident, runaway job, maintenance) stop is a lie. There is also no locking between a foreground `ezgha serve`/`ezgha start` and the service instance: both read `managed_containers()` then spawn the shortfall, so two concurrent processes can each see alive=0 and both spawn `count` runners (2x capacity).
confirmed.recommendation: Make `stop` stop/disable the service (systemctl --user stop ezgha / launchctl unload) or at least detect a running service and refuse with instructions. Add a file lock (e.g. flock on a state file under XDG runtime dir) around ensure_count so serve/start/stop serialize.

verdict.reasoning:
Attempted refutation FAILED — every factual claim verified against the code at /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions.

1. Service resurrects runners after `ezgha stop` — CONFIRMED. src/service.rs:28-38 writes a systemd --user unit with `Restart=on-failure` running `{exe} serve`; src/service.rs:64-78 writes a launchd plist with `KeepAlive=true`. src/main.rs:201-211 is the serve loop: `ensure_count` every 30s, `loop { ... sleep(30s) }`, no exit condition, no stop-signal check. src/main.rs:213-217 (`Commands::Stop`) calls only `docker_backend::stop_all(&cfg)` — it never calls systemctl/launchctl, never signals the serve process, and no stop marker/sentinel file exists. `stop_all` (src/docker_backend.rs:141-156) just does `docker rm -f` on labeled containers and deregisters idle GitHub runners. On the service's next 30s tick, `ensure_count` (docker_backend.rs:187-207) sees alive=0 < count and respawns via fresh JIT configs. Nothing prevents this: the restart config isn't even needed since the serve process never exited. The disk-floor bail (docker_backend.rs:192-199) blocks respawn only when free disk < floor, exactly as the finding says — irrelevant for security/maintenance/runaway-job stop reasons.

2. No durable stop path exists at all — there is no `uninstall-service` command and no mention of disable/uninstall anywhere in src/ or README.md. README.md:29 describes stop as "kill containers, deregister idle runners" without warning of resurrection.

3. No locking — CONFIRMED. grep for lock/flock/pidfile across src/ finds nothing. `ensure_count` is a classic check-then-act: `managed_containers()` count, then spawn shortfall; per-spawn the GitHub JIT-config API call (github::generate_jitconfig in start_one, docker_backend.rs:66-101) happens before `docker run`, giving a multi-second TOCTOU window. A foreground `ezgha serve`/`ezgha start` and the installed service instance can both observe alive=0 and both spawn `count` runners.

4. The e2e evidence bundle (evidence/e2e-20260703: init/doctor/start/status/job-run) never exercises `stop` or the installed service, so it provides no counter-evidence.

Severity: "major" is reasonable, not inflated — a tool whose selling point is host safety/isolation has a stop command that silently fails to stop when the recommended (`install-service` is a headline README feature) deployment mode is used. Not refuted on any ground.

confirmed.title: MANAGED_LABEL is global, not per-target — multi-config hosts miscount capacity and stop kills the wrong runners
confirmed.detail: docker_backend.rs:10, 74-75, 116-138, 141-156. The CLI explicitly supports `--config <path>` (main.rs:22), inviting one host to serve multiple targets (repo A and org B). But every container carries only `ezgha=managed` with no target discriminator, and `managed_containers()` filters solely on that label. Concrete failures: (1) capacity starvation — serve for repo A runs 2 containers; serve for org B (count=2) counts those 2 as its own, sees alive>=count, and never spawns a single runner for B, silently; (2) cross-target kill — `ezgha stop --config B.toml` runs `docker rm -f` on repo A's runner containers, aborting A's in-flight jobs mid-run (docker rm -f during a busy job = job failure on GitHub).
confirmed.recommendation: Add a target-scoped label (e.g. `ezgha.target=<scope>/<target>` or a hash of the config path) at `docker run` time and filter managed_containers()/stop_all() on it.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/docker_backend.rs and main.rs. All cited facts are correct: MANAGED_LABEL="ezgha=managed" (line 10) is the only discriminating label applied (lines 74-76; ezgha.runner_id is a GitHub numeric ID, not a target); managed_containers() (116-138) filters docker ps solely on that label and takes no Config; ensure_count() (188-189) returns early when global alive >= cfg.runner.count, so a second target's serve on the same host spawns zero runners — silently, since Serve prints nothing when started is empty (main.rs:201-211); stop_all() (141-156) runs docker rm -f on ALL managed containers regardless of config target (only the GitHub deregistration half is target-scoped), so `ezgha stop --config B.toml` kills repo A's containers, failing A's in-flight jobs. The --config global flag (main.rs:22) makes the multi-config scenario fully reachable; there is no lock, guard, or per-daemon isolation (no docker-host field in config.rs). Refutation angles rejected: DESIGN.md M4 lists multi-pool as future work but nothing prevents or warns against multi-config use today; both processes share the same docker daemon by default; no earlier error path preempts the starvation. Severity 'major' is reasonable, with the minor caveat that default single-config usage never triggers it — a likelihood note, not a refutation.

confirmed.title: Runner containers omit capability drop, network egress restriction, and read-only rootfs
confirmed.detail: src/docker_backend.rs:79-86 sets --memory/--memory-swap/--cpus/--pids-limit and --security-opt no-new-privileges (plus sysbox runtime when present), but nothing else. Missing: (1) --cap-drop ALL — no-new-privileges only blocks *gaining* new privileges via setuid; it does NOT drop the container's default Linux capability set (CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, NET_RAW, SETUID, SETGID, etc.), so a job retains those. (2) No --network / egress control — the runner has full outbound network and LAN/host-service reachability by default; a compromised job can exfiltrate or pivot. (3) No --read-only rootfs (would need tmpfs for the runner work dir, so it's a larger change, but currently the entire container fs is writable). (4) No explicit --user enforcement (relies on the image's runner uid 1001) and no custom seccomp/AppArmor beyond docker defaults (docker's default seccomp IS applied, which is acceptable). The README 'Security notes' imply a hardened container but do not mention that capabilities are retained, giving a false sense of completeness. Attack scenario: a job on a self-hosted runner that runs partially-trusted code uses retained NET_RAW/default caps plus unrestricted egress to scan the internal network or exfiltrate secrets, none of which the current flags prevent.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `--cap-drop ALL` (adding back only capabilities run.sh genuinely needs), constrain egress (e.g. a dedicated docker network with firewall rules, or --network none where the job model allows), and evaluate --read-only with explicit --tmpfs mounts for the work dir. Update README/DESIGN to state that capabilities and egress are/aren't restricted so the posture claims match the code.

verdict.reasoning:
NOT REFUTED — the factual basis is correct and the core (egress) attack path is reachable — but the finding materially overstates the capability portion and the severity.

Verified facts (all check out):
- /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/src/docker_backend.rs:72-88 (`start_one`) passes exactly: `--memory`, `--memory-swap`, `--cpus`, `--pids-limit`, `--security-opt no-new-privileges`, and `--runtime sysbox-runc` when available. Grep across src/, README.md, DESIGN.md confirms zero occurrences of `--cap-drop`, `--network`, `--read-only`, `--user`, or custom seccomp/AppArmor. The container therefore keeps Docker's default capability bounding set, has full outbound + LAN network via the default bridge, and a writable rootfs. Those factual claims are accurate.
- Egress scenario is real and requires NO capabilities: TCP connect-scans of the LAN and HTTPS exfiltration are fully unprivileged operations, so a compromised job (e.g. a supply-chain-compromised dependency in an otherwise trusted workflow — which is inside even the documented threat model) can pivot/exfiltrate exactly as described. Nothing in the current flags prevents this. This alone sustains the finding.

Overstatements found (severity inflation, not refutation):
1. The headline attack ("uses retained NET_RAW/default caps") is largely NOT exercisable. The configured image (`ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest`, src/config.rs:92 and evidence/e2e-20260703/config.toml:13) has `Config.User=runner` (verified via `docker image inspect` — non-root uid 1001). A non-root process starts with an EMPTY effective/permitted capability set; capabilities exist only in the bounding set. And `no-new-privileges` — which the finding itself acknowledges is applied — blocks the only two re-acquisition paths (setuid binaries like sudo, and file capabilities are ignored under no_new_privs). So the job cannot actually use NET_RAW/CHOWN/DAC_OVERRIDE/SETUID etc.; `--cap-drop ALL` here would be defense-in-depth (shrinks kernel attack surface for a container-escape bug), not closing a live privilege hole. The finding's own text ("no-new-privileges only blocks gaining new privileges... so a job retains those") conflates the bounding set with usable capabilities.
2. The README claim is weak: README.md:62-68 "Security notes" precisely enumerates what IS applied (no-new-privileges, no docker.sock, no privileged mode, cgroup limits) and DESIGN.md has an explicit "Security posture (v1)" plus "Known limitations (v1)" section. Neither claims capability dropping, network isolation, or a hardened rootfs, and both explicitly scope the threat model (no fork-PR jobs, workflow_dispatch/protected branches only, single-user-host tradeoffs). "False sense of completeness" is subjective; the docs do not overpromise.
3. Read-only rootfs is acknowledged by the finding itself as a larger change and is genuinely impractical for a GH Actions runner (jobs write extensively to _work, tool cache, /tmp); its absence is standard for this class of tool, whose stated purpose (docker_backend.rs:77-78 comment, DESIGN.md M1) is resource containment, not sandboxing untrusted code.

Net: factual basis correct, egress scenario occurs, so refuted=false. But "major" is inflated — the exercisable gap is unrestricted egress (a common, medium-severity hardening gap for self-hosted runners, partly mitigated by the documented don't-run-untrusted-code policy); the capability and rootfs items are defense-in-depth nice-to-haves, and the doc-misrepresentation claim does not hold.

confirmed.title: JIT registration credential is exposed on the command line (docker inspect / /proc/*/cmdline) with only a documented 'single-user host' caveat
confirmed.detail: src/docker_backend.rs:88 passes the base64 JIT config as `./run.sh --jitconfig <jit>`. The JIT config is a bearer credential embedding the ephemeral runner's registration/auth; anyone who reads it during the window before the job is picked up can impersonate the runner and receive that job's assignment (source checkout + workflow secrets). It is visible via `docker inspect` (Cmd/Args), the host `docker run` process argv, and `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` for run.sh inside the container. DESIGN.md:81-82 acknowledges this as an 'acceptable single-user-host tradeoff — it is single-use and expires.' The honest evaluation: the mitigation is real but the 'single-user host' assumption is load-bearing and unenforced — the tool also supports org scope and multi-tenant hosts where a co-resident (even unprivileged) process could race to steal the credential. It is single-use and short-lived, which bounds the window, but the argv exposure is strictly worse than a file/env path. Note GitHub's run.sh only accepts --jitconfig as an argument, so the arg form is partly forced.
confirmed.recommendation: Keep the tradeoff but tighten it: (1) prominently document that the argv exposure means the host must be treated as single-tenant/trusted, not just 'single-user'; (2) investigate delivering the jitconfig via a tmpfs-mounted file that run.sh reads, or an env var, to remove it from argv/inspect where the runner image supports it; (3) ensure logs never echo the jit value.

verdict.reasoning: All load-bearing claims verified against source. docker_backend.rs:88 confirmed verbatim: `cmd.args(["./run.sh", "--jitconfig", &jit])` where `jit` is GitHub's base64 `encoded_jit_config` (github.rs:19,57) — a single-use bearer credential embedding the ephemeral runner's registration/auth. Passing it as a positional CLI arg exposes it in the host `docker run` argv (world-readable via `ps` on default Linux), `docker inspect` Cmd/Args, and `/proc/<pid>/cmdline`. DESIGN.md:81-82 confirmed verbatim including the 'acceptable single-user-host tradeoff — it is single-use and expires' quote. The 'single-user host' assumption is genuinely unenforced: config.rs exposes Scope::Org, README documents org scope, and DESIGN.md M4 plans org-scope fleets — so a co-resident unprivileged process could race to read the credential and claim the job (source checkout + workflow secrets) before pickup. The claim that run.sh only accepts --jitconfig as an argument is accurate (stock actions-runner entrypoint), and the finding honestly concedes the arg form is partly forced and the window is bounded. Argv exposure is indeed strictly worse than a file/env path. Severity 'major' is arguably on the high side given the bounded single-use window and the requirement of pre-existing local access plus a race (closer to conditional/medium, gated on multi-tenant deployment), but the finding is explicitly nuanced about these bounds, and per the refutation rule severity inflation alone does not refute. Factual basis correct, scenario can occur in the supported org/multi-tenant mode. Not refuted.

auditor_summaries.summary: Bundle at /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/evidence/e2e-20260703 is SUBSTANTIVELY SOUND but FAILS its own integrity check: sha256sum -c exits 1 because git_sha.txt is listed in checksums.sha256 but absent (critical — the one artifact pinning the bundle to the commit under test is missing). Everything else verified, including against live independent sources: (1) all 10 present files pass checksums; (2) provenance is NOT circular — container ID 1143cf62e74f appears both in local docker status (04) and as Machine name/hostname in GitHub's server-side job log (07), and I independently confirmed via the live GitHub API that run 28685531107 exists (workflow_dispatch, success, head_sha=5ef5406... exactly matching the claimed commit) and that GitHub's jobs endpoint reports runner_name ezgha-Jeff-Ubuntu-6a4833a11c652b with labels [self-hosted, ezgha]; (3) internal math is exact — 5977 MiB * 1048576 = 6,267,338,752 = memory.max in the job log, and 11955 MB daemon / 2 = 5977 matches the daemon-derived-limits design; pids.max 512 matches config and log; timestamps order correctly (run 22:12:21Z → job log 22:12:26-27Z → bundle written ~22:14 UTC); commits 5ef5406 and c27d389 both exist in the repo with matching messages; (4) EVIDENCE.md claims map to artifacts line-by-line except the unbacked 'failure-path cleanup verified' narrative (minor) and the cpu limit, which nproc=4 cannot prove (minor — memory and pids limits ARE proven in-container); (5) nothing smells fabricated — kernel build date day-of-week checks out and the live-API cross-checks would be nearly impossible to fake. Verdict: fix the git_sha.txt checksum failure and this is a PASS-quality bundle; as shipped, the integrity failure caps it at PARTIAL.

Independent live verification of /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/evidence/e2e-20260703/EVIDENCE.md against GitHub — ALL 5 CLAIMS PROVEN, zero mismatches. (1) PROVEN: run 28685531107 on jleechanorg/ez-gh-actions is status=completed, conclusion=success, event=workflow_dispatch, workflowName=ezgha-selftest, headSha=5ef5406779925598565c8d277d13b865db78947a — exactly matching the bundle's commit-under-test. (2) PROVEN: GitHub's own job log for the run contains 'runner name: ezgha-Jeff-Ubuntu-6a4833a11c652b', 'hostname: 1143cf62e74f' (matching the claimed container ID), cgroup memory.max=6267338752 (= 5977 MB exactly), and pids.max=512, followed by the 'ephemeral runner proof' line — all timestamped 2026-07-03T22:12:27Z. (3) PROVEN: gh api repos/jleechanorg/ez-gh-actions/actions/runners returns {"total_count":0,"runners":[]} — the ephemeral JIT-deregistration/cleanup claim holds; no lingering runners. (4) PROVEN: commit 5ef5406779925598565c8d277d13b865db78947a exists on GitHub, and src/docker_backend.rs fetched from GitHub main contains the claimed resource-limit flags at lines 79-82: --memory, --memory-swap, --cpus, --pids-limit. (5) PROVEN: last 3 CI workflow runs on the repo are all conclusion=success, covering headShas 537e1e5, c27d389 (the mid-run daemon-capacity fix commit named in the bundle), and 5ef5406. No critical findings; the evidence bundle is consistent with live GitHub state in every checked dimension.

Hostile review of ez-gh-actions (7 source files + Cargo.toml, ~1160 LOC). Two critical findings: (1) the disk-floor guard — the tool's core safety promise — runs `df` on the HOST against the DAEMON's DockerRootDir path, so it silently fails open (returns None → check skipped) on Colima/Docker Desktop and can read the wrong (host) filesystem while the docker VM disk is full, reproducing the exact disk-fill incident class the tool exists to prevent; (2) `ezgha stop` deregisters ALL idle `ezgha-*` runners on the target, including other hosts' runners in org scope — a fleet-wide outage button. Four majors: `docker ps --format json` is a literal template on Docker CLI <23.0 (Ubuntu 22.04 docker.io) breaking every container operation with a permanent 30s retry loop; crash-looping `--rm` containers leak unbounded orphaned JIT registrations (~2880/day) with no backoff because exited containers are unobservable and start_one only cleans up on docker-run failure; `stop` neither stops the installed serve service (runners resurrect ≤30s) nor holds any lock against concurrent serve/start double-spawning; and the `ezgha=managed` label is not target-scoped, so multi-config hosts miscount capacity (one target silently starves) and stop kills the other target's in-flight jobs. Eight minors with concrete scenarios: no runner-list pagination (>100 runners), clamping limits to 100% of daemon capacity (VM OOM), systemd --user needs enable-linger on headless hosts (+ ineffective After=docker.service), unvalidated hostname breaking the 64-char runner-name limit (perpetual 422 loop on FQDN hosts), collision-prone unique_suffix (no PID/randomness, ambiguous concat), stop_all reporting false success and deregistering runners whose containers survived, JIT credentials exposed in argv/docker inspect, and init silently clobbering tuned configs plus unvalidated org targets. Cargo.toml itself is clean.

Reviewed all 6 Rust sources and both workflows. No shell is used (Command arg-vectors), and gh label/name args are safely constructed with -f. Public-repo posture is sound: selftest.yml is workflow_dispatch-only and ci.yml runs on ubuntu-latest with pull_request (not pull_request_target), so forks never reach a self-hosted runner. Four findings: (1) MAJOR — cfg.runner.image is a bare positional in `docker run`, so a leading-dash value (e.g. --privileged, --network=host, -v=/:/host) is parsed as a docker flag and silently defeats every hardening flag; needs input validation (docker run has no `--` guard before IMAGE). (2) MAJOR — container hardening omits `--cap-drop ALL` (default caps are retained; no-new-privileges does not drop them), network egress restriction, and read-only rootfs; the no-new-privileges + cgroup + no-docker.sock claims are accurate but the README overstates completeness. (3) MAJOR — the JIT credential is passed on the command line (visible to docker inspect / /proc cmdline); the documented 'single-user host' tradeoff is honest but the assumption is unenforced and load-bearing, especially for org scope. (4) MINOR — service.rs interpolates the exe path into the systemd unit and launchd plist without XML-escaping/validation (content-injection, gated by binary-path control). Files: src/docker_backend.rs, src/github.rs, src/service.rs, src/config.rs.
